original_hypothesis.title: BondingTax swapForAsset Sandwich Attack via Spot Price Slippage
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The `swapForAsset` function in `BondingTax` calculates slippage protection using the current spot price (`router.getAmountsOut`). Since this function is triggered on every bonding curve trade, MEV bots can sandwich the trades to manipulate the spot price and steal accumulated tax revenue.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Relying on spot prices for slippage in automated swaps is a classic MEV vulnerability. The predictable trigger (every trade) makes it highly exploitable for continuous profit extraction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: minOutput is derived directly from router.getAmountsOut(amount, path) in the same transaction

preconditions: feeTo is set to taxManager in FFactory
Sufficient taxToken accumulated to cover gas costs

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: expectedOutput is manipulated via front-running pool skew
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker buys taxToken cheap from the protocol swap, then sells it back at normal price

economic_questions: How much taxToken must accumulate to make the sandwich profitable?
What is the optimal pool skew to maximize extraction?
validation_requirements: Simulate a flash loan sandwich attack on a bonding curve trade
Calculate net profit after Uniswap fees

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 99% of accumulated tax tokens per trade
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High, limited only by pool depth and fees

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When taxToken balance in BondingTax is high

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify getAmountsOut behavior under extreme skew

validation_result.mechanism: Spot-price-derived slippage in automated tax swap
validation_result.impact_summary: BondingTax.swapForAsset uses same-transaction spot price (router.getAmountsOut) to derive minOutput, providing no meaningful protection against sandwich attacks on accumulated tax revenue.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The swapForAsset function in BondingTax.sol queries the external AMM router's getAmountsOut to determine expectedOutput, then applies a 1% slippage tolerance to calculate minOutput. Since getAmountsOut reflects the current pool reserves which can be manipulated within the same block, an attacker can front-run to skew the pool, causing getAmountsOut to return a depressed value. The minOutput is then set to 99% of this already-manipulated price, providing virtually no protection. The function is automatically triggered on every bonding curve buy/sell when taxVault == taxManager, making the timing predictable for MEV bots.

The swap is performed through an external AMM (ASSET_ROUTER per deployment args), not the internal FRouter. This means an attacker manipulates the external AMM pool to extract value from the tax swap.

The correct pattern would be to use a TWAP oracle, or have the minOutput calculated off-chain and supplied as a parameter (as AgentTax.handleAgentTaxes does with its minOutput parameter).
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 85-88 of BondingTax.sol confirm the pattern: getAmountsOut is called in the same transaction as the swap, and the result is used as the basis for slippage calculation. The _slippage default of 100 (1%) at line 63 of initialize only protects against price movement between the getAmountsOut call and the swap within the same transaction—not against pre-manipulation of the pool state.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: getAmountsOut returns the current spot price from the AMM pool. An attacker who front-runs this transaction can manipulate the pool reserves so that getAmountsOut returns a much lower value. The minOutput is then 99% of this already-manipulated value, providing no real slippage protection.
swapForAsset is triggered automatically on every bonding curve sell when taxVault == taxManager, making the timing completely predictable for MEV searchers.
swapForAsset is also triggered on every bonding curve buy when taxVault == taxManager, doubling the attack surface.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls Bonding.buy(amountIn, tokenAddress) or Bonding.sell(amountIn, tokenAddress)
2. Bonding calls FRouter.buy() or FRouter.sell() (EXECUTOR_ROLE gated)
3. FRouter computes fee via factory.buyTax()/sellTax() and sends txFee to feeTo=factory.taxVault()
4. FRouter checks if feeTo == taxManager; if so, calls IBondingTax(taxManager).swapForAsset()
5. BondingTax.swapForAsset reads taxToken balance, queries router.getAmountsOut for spot price
6. minOutput derived as (expectedOutput * (10000 - 100)) / 10000 = 99% of spot quote
7. Calls router.swapExactTokensForTokens with this minOutput to swap taxToken for assetToken on external AMM
8. MEV bot front-runs step 5-7: manipulates external AMM pool, causing getAmountsOut to return depressed value
9. Swap executes at the manipulated price with inadequate minOutput
10. MEV bot back-runs: reverses pool manipulation, extracts profit
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability exists because getAmountsOut (BondingTax.sol line ~85) and swapExactTokensForTokens (BondingTax.sol line ~89) both interact with the same external AMM pool state in the same transaction. An attacker who manipulates the pool state before this transaction causes both the price query and the slippage limit to reflect the manipulated state, rendering the slippage protection ineffective.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: arguments/bondingTax.js shows router_ = process.env.ASSET_ROUTER (external AMM) and bondingRouter_ = process.env.FROUTER (internal). The _slippage is hardcoded to 100 in initialize (BondingTax.sol line 63).

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is feasible: (1) swapForAsset is called predictably on every bonding curve trade when taxVault == taxManager - this is deterministic from mempool monitoring; (2) the external AMM pool used for the swap can be manipulated via flash loans or large trades; (3) getAmountsOut returns the manipulated spot price; (4) minOutput is 99% of the manipulated (depressed) price, not the fair price; (5) the swap executes at the bad price; (6) attacker reverses their manipulation for profit. The maxSwapThreshold limits the amount per swap but doesn't prevent the attack, it just caps the loss per transaction. The try/catch means a failed swap doesn't revert the bonding curve trade but the tax tokens remain for the next attack attempt.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The protocol's bonding curve tax revenue is continuously drained by MEV sandwich attacks. Every bonding curve trade that triggers swapForAsset creates an opportunity. The impact scales with: (a) accumulated tax tokens in BondingTax contract, (b) depth of the external AMM pool, (c) the maxSwapThreshold setting. With default 1% slippage, an attacker can extract up to ~99% of the fair swap value (minus AMM fees and gas costs) by sufficiently skewing the pool.
recommended_mitigations: Use a TWAP oracle instead of spot price for slippage calculation
Accept minOutput as a parameter from an off-chain oracle (similar to how AgentTax.handleAgentTaxes accepts minOutput from EXECUTOR_ROLE)
Add a configurable minimum absolute output floor that cannot be bypassed by spot price manipulation
Consider using Chainlink or similar oracle for price reference
Implement a circuit breaker that pauses swaps if the spot price deviates significantly from a stored reference price
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy BondingTax with an AeroAdaptor/UniswapV2 router pointed at a test pool. 2. Accumulate taxTokens in BondingTax above minSwapThreshold. 3. In a single block: (a) attacker dumps a large amount of assetToken into the AMM pool to crash the taxToken price; (b) trigger a bonding curve trade that calls swapForAsset; (c) attacker buys back assetToken at the depressed price. 4. Measure the difference between fair swap output and actual output. 5. Compare attacker profit against gas + AMM fees to confirm profitability.
uncertainties: The exact external AMM router implementation (Uniswap V2, Aerodrome, etc.) affects pool manipulation costs
The maxSwapThreshold and minSwapThreshold values are set via environment variables and not visible—they bound the maximum loss per swap
Pool depth of the taxToken/assetToken pair determines manipulation cost and attacker capital requirements
additional_testing_needed: Determine actual deployed values for minSwapThreshold and maxSwapThreshold
Identify which external AMM pool is used and its typical liquidity depth
Calculate minimum profitable attack size given AMM fees and gas costs on Base chain
Verify that taxVault is indeed set equal to taxManager in production (needed for swapForAsset to be triggered)
